_instructions.description: This file contains all articles for the Simply Nerdy website.
_instructions.howToAdd: To add a new article, copy the template below and add it to the 'posts' array. Posts are displayed in reverse chronological order (newest first).

template.title: Your Article Title
template.category: One of: Games, Books, Movies, TV Shows, Music
template.excerpt: A brief 1-2 sentence summary that appears on article cards
template.content:
Full HTML content with

paragraphs,

headings,
  • lists, emphasis, bold, etc.
template.author: Simply Nerdy
template.image: Path to featured image in /images/articles/ folder (e.g., '/images/articles/article-name.jpg')

contentTips: Use <h4> for main section headings within the article
Use <em> for italic text and <strong> for bold
Keep the content engaging and conversational
Images should be 16:9 aspect ratio, ideally 1600x900px or 1920x1080px
Place article images in the /images/articles/ folder with descriptive filenames
To add images within content:
For images with captions:
_instructions.tagGuidelines: Use 3-5 relevant tags per post. Tags help with search and discovery.

posts.title: Banjo-Kazooie Review
posts.excerpt: Banjo-Kazooie is more than a nostalgic classic — it's a masterclass in platformer design that still holds up nearly 30 years later, and one of the most joyful games ever made.
posts.content:
It’s Summer of 1998, only two years after the release of the Nintendo 64. As an 11-year-old Nintendo fanboy, I rush to meet a friend in the neighborhood three doors down who is claiming to have the next big game. He shows me something being called “the new Mario 64”—and none of us were prepared for how accurate that statement would turn out to be. The game is called Banjo-Kazooie: a brown bear in yellow shorts who plays a banjo and carries a blue backpack holding his friend Kazooie, a vulgar red bird who is crass and sarcastic toward absolutely everyone you come across.

I was instantly hooked by the vibrant colors and personality of Rare’s first 3D platformer. Lucky for me, my birthday was several weeks away—and through the power of childhood persuasion, I convinced my mom to buy it as an early present. The rest is history.

A Cartoon Inside a Video Game

Banjo-Kazooie is a cartoon in a video game. The joyous style and message jumps off the screen from start to finish. Everything in this game is alive—no, really. Everything. Feathers, honeycombs, lightbulbs—they’re all sentient and conscious. Every object has eyeballs and communicates through a hilariously expressive chant of sounds, chirps, and whistles. An orange makes squishy, wet sound effects. A hippo-pirate character constantly burps and belches, guaranteed to make any 10-year-old howl with laughter.

But don’t let the whimsy fool you. Beneath the cartoon surface lies something closer to a dark German fairy tale. The antagonist is an evil green witch named Gruntilda who kidnaps Banjo’s little sister to steal her youth and transform herself into a beautiful witch ready to pick up some dudes. No, seriously. That’s the actual plot.

Nine Worlds of Unforgettable Design

To save his sister, Banjo must collect Jigsaw pieces—Jiggies—scattered throughout Grunty’s massive lair. The nine worlds are brimming with identity and unforgettable music. Just Google “Bubblegloop Swamp theme” if you need convincing.

I still remember my first experience nearly 30 years ago: swimming through a deep underwater tunnel only to come face to face with an enormous chained metal shark named Clanker—Grunty’s garbage disposal. You eventually venture inside the cyborg shark through a cavernous space of flesh and spinning blades, like surviving inside a giant living sink. From snow to swamp to sand, every world has its own identity. But the crown jewel is Tick Tock Woods—a level that cycles through all four seasons, shifting water levels, letting you feed an eagle and watch it grow, help a squirrel gather nuts for hibernation, and even transform into a bee to explore a hive. It’s one of the greatest platforming stages ever designed.

The Lair That Ties It All Together

Grunty’s Lair is the pinnacle of hub world design. As you advance, unlock secrets, and collect Notes and Jinjos—those weird, vibrant birdlike creatures scattered throughout—the lair itself evolves. Each new world bleeds its musical influence into the lair’s score, layering the soundtrack as you progress. The music in this game is so iconic that you can ask almost any fan to hum a world’s theme and they’ll remember nearly all of them. That’s not nostalgia talking—that’s exceptional composition.

One of Gaming’s Greatest Finales

The game ends on one of the most creative and memorable finales in gaming history. Rare had the audacity to challenge your memory by quizzing you on the entire scope of the game through a full-on game show—hosted by Gruntilda herself. You cannot confidently pass this test unless you’ve actually played the game. Even 28 years later, I was grinning ear to ear when the challenge came around again.

After the game show comes the final battle atop Grunty’s high, spooky tower—a fight requiring you to call on nearly every ability you’ve learned, while Grunty tries to plow into you on her grinning broom, beating it like she’s trying to start a stubborn car. And speaking of Gruntilda—she is one of the best video game antagonists ever made. She mocks you throughout the entire game, rhyming every single time she opens her mouth. Her own sister Brentilda spoils her lifestyle in absurd little facts along the way: Grunty washes her hair with engine oil. Grunty enjoys a smoothie of elephant sweat. She is ridiculous in the absolute best way.

A Core Memory, Forever

One of my core memories as a kid was finally defeating Gruntilda—not just for the wild adventure, and not even because of the pain and hardship of collecting every Note in the dreaded waters of Rusty Bucket Bay (all fans will understand this one). It was because my two brothers and I got to witness the resurrection of a Mega Jinjo brought forth to end Grunty’s reign, pummeling her senselessly in glorious, exaggerated cartoon fashion. The Mega Jinjo, in a thunderous deep voice, bellows “JINJO!!!” with the final blow and sends her flying off the tower into the abyss. We laughed until it hurt.

Banjo-Kazooie defined a generation. It swept Mario 64 aside and proclaimed itself the definitive masterpiece of 90’s platformers—a hallmark of Nintendo and Rare excellence, and a paragon of polished game design. I will play Banjo-Kazooie forever. It isn’t held together by nostalgia alone; it became a part of me. As Kazooie herself says: “Great, I get to peck some more witch butt! Let’s go, Banjo!”

Final Score

10/10
posts.author: Lakez
